quick_summary: Bash is the default shell on Linux and macOS, essential for system administration, CI/CD scripting, and DevOps automation. It matters for interviews because every backend, DevOps, and SRE role requires shell scripting — you'll be tested on piping, text processing, and writing reliable automation scripts.

detailed_content.explanation:
Bash is the default shell on Linux and macOS. Every command follows the pattern: command [options] [arguments]. The shell expands wildcards, redirects output, and pipes between commands. The filesystem is a tree rooted at / — you navigate with cd, list with ls, and inspect with pwd.

The real power of Bash comes from combining simple tools via pipes. `cat file | grep ERROR | wc -l` counts error lines in a log file. Each tool does one thing well — `cat` reads files, `grep` filters lines, `wc` counts. This Unix philosophy means you can solve complex problems by chaining small, reliable commands. Redirection (`>`, `>>`, `2>&1`) controls where output goes — files, other commands, or /dev/null to discard it.
detailed_content.example:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# System health check script

LOG_FILE="/var/log/app/errors.log"
REPORT="/tmp/health_report.txt"

{
    echo "=== Health Report $(date) ==="
    echo ""
    echo "--- Disk Usage ---"
    df -h / | tail -1 | awk '{print $5 " used on " $6}'
    
    echo ""
    echo "--- Memory ---"
    free -h | grep Mem | awk '{print $3 "/" $2 " used"}'
    
    echo ""
    echo "--- Recent Errors ---"
    if [ -f "$LOG_FILE" ]; then
        tail -20 "$LOG_FILE" | grep -c "ERROR" | xargs -I{} echo "{} errors in last 20 lines"
    else
        echo "Log file not found"
    fi
    
    echo ""
    echo "--- Top 5 CPU Processes ---"
    ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -6
} > "$REPORT" 2>&1

# Email if disk > 80%
DISK_PCT=$(df / | tail -1 | awk '{print $5}' | tr -d '%')
if [ "$DISK_PCT" -gt 80 ]; then
    echo "WARNING: Disk at ${DISK_PCT}%" | mail -s "Disk Alert" admin@company.com
fi

echo "Report saved to $REPORT"
```
detailed_content.key_takeaway: Bash power comes from chaining simple tools with pipes — `cat | grep | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn` solves 90% of log analysis problems. Master these five commands and you can handle most automation tasks.
detailed_content.try_it: Write a one-liner that finds the top 10 most common error messages in /var/log/syslog, sorted by frequency. Hint: use grep, awk, sort, uniq, and head.
detailed_content.complexity: Time: O(n log n) for sort-based pipelines, Space: O(n) for buffering

pro_tip: In interviews, always add `set -euo pipefail` at the top of bash scripts — it makes scripts fail fast on errors (`-e`), undefined variables (`-u`), and pipe failures (`-o pipefail`). This single line separates production-ready scripts from dangerous ones.
